evidence.title: Clinical Guidance for Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis | Group A Strep | CDC
evidence.snippet: Cephalexin, oral 20 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 500 mg/dose) for 10 days Cefadroxil, oral 30 mg/kg once daily (maximum = 1 g) for 10 days Clindamycin, oral 7 mg/kg/dose 3 times daily (maximum = 300 mg/dose) for 10 days Azithromycin, oral 12 mg/kg once (maximum = 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg (max=250 mg) once daily for the next 4 days Clarithromycin, oral 7.5 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 250 mg/dose) for 10 days Antibiotic resistance There's never been a report of a clinical isolate of group A strep bacteria that's resistant to penicillin or cephalosporins.

evidence.title: Strep throat: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Certain other antibiotics may also work against the strep bacteria. Antibiotics should be taken for 10 days, even though symptoms are often gone within a few days. The following tips may help your sore throat feel better:

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 2-day history of sore throat accompanied by fever, chills, swollen lymph nodes, and white patches on the tonsils. She denies cough, nasal congestion, runny nose, difficulty swallowing, or breathing. She reports recent exposure to strep throat through her son last week. She has tried tea with honey and NyQuil without relief. There is no history of rheumatic fever, heart valve disease, or kidney problems. She has a penicillin allergy and is not pregnant.

O: Phone encounter only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The clinical presentation is consistent with streptococcal pharyngitis (strep throat), given the fever, tonsillar exudates, swollen lymph nodes, and known exposure.
Differential:
1. Streptococcal pharyngitis
2. Viral pharyngitis
3. Infectious mononucleosis
4. Other bacterial pharyngitis

P: - First-line medication: Azithromycin 500 mg orally on day 1, then 250 mg orally daily on days 2 through 5 (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Clindamycin 300 mg orally three times daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: Patient has a penicillin allergy; avoid beta-lactam antibiotics. Monitor for gastrointestinal side effects with macrolides and clindamycin. Other contraindications not assessed in this telephone encounter.
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if difficulty breathing or swallowing develops, or if symptoms worsen or fail to improve within 48-72 hours.
- Testing: Arrange for rapid antigen detection test or throat culture for Group A Streptococcus if possible to confirm diagnosis and guide therapy.
